title: When Anger Mourns: Grieving the Funeral You Couldn't Attend
dek: You missed the funeral - the final ritual of farewell - and now your body thrums with a fierce, ragged anger that twists through your diaphragm and scrapes along your jaw. This is not denial or weakness; this is the body anchoring grief where words haven’t landed. In this piece, we honor that anger as a vital messenger and companion on the jagged edge of loss, weaving somatic wisdom from Karla McLaren and the tender mindfulness teachings of Thich Nhat Hanh.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Body's Betrayal: Missing the Funeral, Carrying the Rage


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:42.979Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

The night carries its own gravity - three o’clock, when the world has sucked all warmth from the air and your body wakes in silent witness. You lie flat on your back, the sheets cool against your skin, but inside, something clenches down with the precision of a ritual you did not sign up for. Your masseter muscles - those jawbones twisting just beneath the hollow of your cheeks - lock tight as if trying to muffle a scream that will not come. That grinding tenacity, that buried fury, is not some abstract “anger.” It is your body remembering the moment it could not show up, the funeral you missed, the grief unspoken in a room you never entered.

Your trapezius muscles clutch the weight of this absence. From the base of your skull, they climb like iron rails down your neck and across your shoulder blades, shortening and thickening as if to barricade your chest against the ache of what you lost - and how you lost it. You feel the levator scapulae murmuring along the sides of your neck, each twitch a subtle reminder that your body is not merely suffering but recording this grief into its very architecture. This is not just emotional pain you can think away. This is fascia tightening, a biological memory that curves your spine forward in a half-hunch as if shielding your heart from a world that refused you entry.

Thich Nhat Hanh once pointed to the body as the door to presence: “When we touch the body in mindful awareness, we touch the feet of the Buddha.” But this mindful awareness doesn't erase the ache; it honors its shape. And here, in the midnight stillness, your body is talking in the language of the diaphragm, where tension nests just beneath your ribs. You might notice your breath caught high, climbing shallow and fast as if your lungs are trying, in a cruel imitation of life, to hold back grief that has no outlet. Pema Chödrön calls this the edge of emotional flood - where anger meets sorrow in the body and spills over into the long, hard night.

Robert Augustus Masters taught that anger is the guardian of a breached boundary, a fierce sentinel to losses that felt like betrayals. Your body is that sentinel now, wielding the sharpness of clenched fists inside your belly, the tightening of psoas muscles - the body’s deep stabilizer anchored in your pelvis - each knot a coil of frustration that you couldn’t sit by the coffin, say the names aloud, shed tears alongside others. This is not pathology, this is biology. Your body’s rage at your absence is its way of carrying what words could not hold.

The scent of your skin in the dark holds this memory too - the subtle saltiness of sweat, the faint musk that rises when muscles tense, all mingling with the night air through the cracked window. It's a pheromone story of power withheld and presence denied. Resmaa Menakem reminds us that trauma is stored not just in the mind, but in the flesh beneath the skin - the voluntary muscles that clench with history. You carry this too, invisible yet insistent.

When you cannot attend the funeral, your body attends instead. And in its tight bands and restless pulses, the anger you were told to hide is raw, vivid, honest. This is your body’s betrayal - not a failure, but an unyielding record. It knows what the mind still tries to deny: absence is a wound, and your muscles remember every centimeter of that loss.

## Cultural Scripts and the Forbidden Fury of Grief

This is the weight settling deep into the psoas, the muscle that holds not only your core but your capacity to open and contract with the ups and downs of grief. When you couldn’t stand by the casket, couldn’t feel the solid presence of flesh and bone with family and friends, something tightened there. Your body, your lived experience, insists on a story that the outside world refuses to see. You aren’t just holding space; you’re holding the refusal.

Resmaa Menakem, who writes so compellingly about how trauma pulses through the body of Black Americans, makes clear that grief isn’t merely personal - it’s policed. It’s fenced in by cultural scripts that dictate what feelings can be worn like a badge of honor and which must be hidden beneath layers of shame and self-censorship. When you didn’t attend the funeral, the message from those scripts may be as sharp and silent as the ridge of your jaw clenching. You feel the masseter tense, your breath shallow in the diaphragm, but the world won’t validate the rage bubbling beneath because it wasn’t “proper” - didn’t come with the expected public rites, the communal release. You’re left holding furious fragments in a room no one invited you to.

Menakem’s insight into racialized trauma teaches us the body itself becomes the battleground for these unspoken rules. The levator scapulae - those muscles that lift your shoulders towards your ears - swell with the tension of silent grief, your neck muscles bracing against a world that tells you to suck it up, that anger in mourning is unseemly or disrespects the dead. You’re taught that your fury over absence is ugly, dangerous even, an impolite disturbance in the delicate choreography of social mourning. Your bones remember standing outside the line, your fascia remembering the sharp exclusion, the internalized judgment.

This isn’t just about you or your particular loss. It’s about how bodies coded as Other carry grief differently, how the template for ‘proper mourning’ skews towards a sanitized, emotionless performance that erases messy, raging realities. The visceral ache that tightens your trapezius at the base of your neck? That’s the body screaming for the grief you weren’t given space to show. And when the body remembers, your mind is caught in a gnarly knot: carrying the rage but needing to hide it, knowing it’s rightful but forbidden.

The scripts say you must hold this fury inside, like a secret brand pressed against ribs and sternum. But this internal war - between feeling and showing - renders the anger not a refusal but a reclamation. The body echoing Menakem’s teachings, tells you that grief in absence is not absence of grief. It’s a silence so sharp it cuts through skin and muscle and ligaments, demanding recognition even when the world has turned away. When your body rattles in the quiet hours, your jaw grinding down on molars like a makeshift funeral bell, listen. This is not pathology, this is biology. Your angers mourn. Your body's memory is a reckoning, and it insists that your fury, far from being shameful, has been waiting patiently for your permission to roar.

## The Jaw Remembers: Somatic Wisdom in the Face of Unseen Farewells

You sit in a quiet room, perhaps late in the blue folds of a night just before dawn. The air is still but for the faint hum of a city that has long since gone to sleep. Your masseter muscles - the twin workhorses of your jaw - feel like stone gates clamped tight. There’s a rigidness here, a knotted resistance beneath your cheekbones, where the temporalis fans out like a secret map of clenched stories. These aren’t just muscles holding your teeth together; they are repositories of memories. The soft fascia around them tightens like old wounds pressed beneath the veneer of civility. This is not just tension - it is your body’s way of saying it remembers the funeral you couldn’t attend.

Robert Augustus Masters teaches that the body refuses to forget what the mind is still trying to silence. You can sit with the replay of that absence - the empty seat, the hollow space at the table - and your mind may attempt to shove the ache into a tiny pocket labeled “too painful.” But your body, especially your jaw, refuses such tidy compartmentalization. It tightens the masseter in quiet protest, the temporalis twitches in spasms you hardly notice but are relentless. This is somatic wisdom crystallizing in muscle fibers and fascia, the body’s encrypted journal of grief and fury entwined.

You notice your breath hitching as well, a subtle diaphragm spasm that feels like a hiccup without sound. The diaphragm, that thick domed muscle beneath your ribcage, often bristles or short-circuits in grief. It catches like a stubborn bird trapped behind the glass of your sternum. This isn’t a metaphor. It’s your body’s biology responding to emotional rupture - a diaphragm refusing to smooth its arc because it is holding a weight no one saw when you said you couldn’t go. That hitch repeats in the hollow of your ribs, catching your breath in a way that leaves you gasping for closure you never had the chance to claim.

Masters reminds us that somatic psychology isn’t about willfully making pain vanish but about leaning into the cellular memory of our bodies. The jaw remembers more than your rational mind admits: the refusal to hold space at the funeral becomes an unspoken sentence, and your muscles - the masseter and temporalis - wear that sentence like a badge. Your diaphragm’s spasms are the silent sobbing that never found sound, the unvoiced fury at circumstance and silence. 

In feeling this, you step away from the tyranny of intellectualizing grief or trying to be the “strong” mourner who doesn’t break. Instead, you allow the body’s whispers to speak in spasms and clenches. You might even notice a faint metallic scent of adrenaline mingling with the faintest trace of something sweeter - perhaps a subtle herbal musk from dried chamomile tucked near the windowsill - a beginning salve for the rawness held within your fascia.

This remembrance in muscle is your body’s insistence that the grief, the anger, and the loss remain visible somewhere, even if your mind tried to fold them into silence. The jaw remembers, the diaphragm convulses. This is not a failing but proof that your grief, though unseen at the funeral you missed, is still deeply, somatically honored within you.

## Anger as Messenger: Listening Beyond Moralizing

Your anger isn’t just noise. It’s the electric hum of a muscle - maybe the tense, unyielding clench of your masseter, or the tight, immovable knot in your levator scapulae - that speaks in a language beyond words. When you miss the funeral you couldn’t attend, your body wants what the mind refuses: acknowledgment, presence, a ritual held in the soft, trembling cradle of your chest. Karla McLaren reminds us that anger is a messenger, not a monster. It is the guardian of your boundaries, the fierce voice that insists you be seen and heard - especially when the rituals you rely on, the shared laments and collective tears, are snatched away by circumstance.

This fury pulses through your fascia like a call to attention. The connective tissue, dense around your ribs and diaphragm, tightens with each breath held too long. It’s not spite or guilt. It’s biology rewriting your story: there was a wound left uncovered, a door slammed before you could close it yourself. When McLaren sketches anger as the protector of your inner borders, she gives you permission to feel its full shape - not as an ugly thing to shove down, but as a complex compass pointing straight to the edges of what was robbed from you.

You might notice this anger in the tremor that rides the trapezius, or in the sudden catch of breath when a kind word or a forgotten photo brings it up from beneath your skin. This is your grief, fully embodied, demanding witness. It is not a failure to experience it this way. Rather, this visceral unrest reveals the depth of your love and the disruptions around your loss. Karla’s work teaches you that anger speaks in unmet needs. The need to stand beside your kin, to fold your hands in prayer or to let your tears mingle with others’ - these needs embroidered into your nervous system now scream beneath your skin.

So instead of apologizing for your ire or trying to tame it prematurely, lean into its texture. Feel the spread of tension across the zygomatic arch or the steady burn of the psoas that tightens whenever you remember the day you stayed away. Your body knows the gaps in your grieving - the spaces where the ritual should have been - but anger fills those spaces with urgent, living energy. It insists on acknowledgment without shame. It’s not about moralizing what you “should” feel or how “right” your response is; it’s about whittling anger down to its raw message and listening deeply until the grief beneath it can finally unfold.

This is not pathology. This is biology honoring what your eyes did not get to witness, what your arms could not hold, and what your community could not enfold you in. Anger holds the line between survival and surrender, inviting you to meet your loss with your whole body, not just your mind. In that fierce, unvarnished presence, you start to see what the anger guards - and what it is trying to protect: your right to grieve, however fractured or incomplete, fully lived in the sinew, muscle, and breath of your being.

## The Interrupted Ritual: What the Funeral Meant, What Its Absence Means Now

You carry the ache of absence deep in your body, a hollow where the funeral should have been. That gathering - the ritual itself - was much more than a ceremony. It was the deliberate breaking of one story and the tender weaving of another, a collective exhale punctuated by tears, prayers, and the unmistakable undercurrent of shared loss. When you couldn’t attend, something vital ruptured. This rupture isn’t just psychological; it’s lodged in the tissue, the fascia that holds you together, the levator scapulae and chest tight as a clenched fist refusing to release.

Funerals exist not merely to mark death but to tend the soil of transition. They are ancient rites of passage crafted by cultures that knew the body remembers even when words fail. That gathered crowd - faces wet with grief - creates a container for sorrow, permitting the individual heart to crack open safely. In that container, the chest may tighten and the diaphragm catch as the collective heartbeat slows and then pulses again, steady and real. The tears that flow lubricate memories stored in muscle and memory, allowing the body to soften the edge of loss. You were meant to be there, shoulders rounded, jaw a little slack as you exhale the unfinished story. Instead, your levator scapulae knots in protest - those ribbons of muscle from the neck to the scapula tensing as if guarding a secret too big to name.

Teachers like Resmaa Menakem remind us that trauma - a funeral missed is a form of trauma - resides in the body’s tissue, not only in the mind’s archive. This is not pathology; it’s biology. Your chest fascia tightens, cells sending distress signals as they grip, trapping the grief that refuses to flow. Without witnessing the communal acknowledgment of death, you lose the chance to externalize your pain - to say, “this death shapes me” in a space where you are held by others mourning too. Thich Nhat Hanh’s invitations to “call the spirit of your grief back” become difficult when you’ve no shared altar or open grave to direct that call toward.

The absence of this ritual leaves you stranded in a liminal space. The body, denied its public mourning, turns inward. The anger you feel - the sharp bite in your jaw, the heavy drag in your psoas - is the body’s attempt to create its own ritual of resistance. It is a fierce, silent protest against the interruption of your grief. You are mourning not just the person who died, but the opportunity to say goodbye in the way your body expected. The interrupted ritual becomes a ghost in your muscles, a phantom limb of grief that aches when the weather turns cold or when the anniversary of the death rolls around.

To heal this, you must acknowledge the interruption. You must honor the anger that guards the empty space where the funeral should have been. This means allowing the levator scapulae to hold its tension without judgment, recognizing it as a monument to the unsaid. It means breathing into the tight chest fascia, not to force it open, but to sit with it in the dark, offering the body the witness it was denied. The ritual may have been interrupted, but the body’s capacity to mourn - and to heal - remains intact, waiting for you to listen.

## Mindful Witnessing: Thich Nhat Hanh and the Art of Breathing Into Anger

When the anger rises - when the masseter clenches and the diaphragm hitches - your instinct may be to turn away, to distract yourself, or to force the tension down. But Thich Nhat Hanh offers a different path: the art of mindful witnessing. He teaches that anger is not an enemy to be vanquished, but a crying baby that needs to be held. “Breathing in, I know that anger is in me. Breathing out, I am taking good care of my anger.” This is not a passive surrender; it is an active, embodied embrace of the fury that lives in your fascia.

To practice this, you must first locate the anger in your body. Where does it live? Is it the tight band across your chest, the knot in your psoas, the rigid line of your jaw? Once you find it, do not try to change it. Simply breathe into it. Let the breath be a gentle hand resting on the tense muscle. As you inhale, acknowledge the presence of the anger. As you exhale, offer it your presence. This is the essence of mindful witnessing: to be with the anger without being consumed by it, to hold it in the spaciousness of your awareness.

Thich Nhat Hanh reminds us that anger is often rooted in deep suffering. When you missed the funeral, you suffered a profound loss - not just of the person who died, but of the opportunity to mourn them in community. The anger that arises is a natural response to this suffering. By breathing into it, you are acknowledging the depth of your pain. You are saying to your body, “I see you. I hear you. I am here with you.” This simple act of recognition can begin to soften the edges of the tension, allowing the grief beneath the anger to surface.

This practice requires patience and courage. It is not easy to sit with the raw, unvarnished truth of your anger. But as you continue to breathe into it, you may notice a shift. The masseter may begin to unclench, the diaphragm may release its grip, the chest fascia may soften. The anger may not disappear entirely, but it will lose its sharp edge. It will become a companion rather than a tyrant, a guide rather than an obstacle. Through mindful witnessing, you can transform the energy of anger into the energy of compassion - for yourself, for the person you lost, and for the interrupted ritual that left you stranded in grief.

## Embodied Practice: Releasing the Funeral Anger Held in Your Body

The anger you carry from the missed funeral is not just an emotion; it is a physical weight, a knot of tension lodged in your muscles and fascia. To release it, you must engage the body directly. This embodied practice is designed to help you access and release the anger held in your masseter, levator scapulae, and diaphragm. It is a gentle, somatic approach that honors the body’s wisdom and invites the tension to unwind at its own pace.

Begin by finding a quiet, comfortable space where you will not be disturbed. You may want to have a cup of [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20) tea nearby, as this herb is known for its ability to soothe the nervous system and ease muscular tension. Sit or lie down in a position that feels supportive. Close your eyes and take a few deep breaths, allowing your body to settle into the space. Notice any areas of tension or discomfort, particularly in your jaw, neck, and chest.

Focus your attention on your masseter muscles - the thick muscles at the back of your jaw. Notice if they are clenched or tight. Without trying to force them to relax, simply bring your awareness to this area. Imagine a warm, soothing light bathing your jaw, gently melting away the tension. You may want to gently massage the masseter muscles with your fingertips, using small, circular motions. As you do this, allow your jaw to drop slightly, creating space between your upper and lower teeth.

Next, bring your attention to your levator scapulae - the muscles that run from the base of your skull down to your shoulder blades. Notice if your shoulders are hunched or elevated. Again, without forcing anything, imagine the warm light spreading down your neck and into your shoulders. You may want to gently roll your shoulders back and down, or gently stretch your neck from side to side. As you do this, imagine the tension melting away, leaving your neck and shoulders feeling soft and relaxed.

Finally, focus on your diaphragm - the large muscle beneath your ribcage that controls your breathing. Notice if your breath is shallow or restricted. Place one hand on your belly and the other on your chest. As you inhale, imagine your breath expanding your belly like a balloon. As you exhale, imagine the breath releasing any tension or anger held in your diaphragm. Continue to breathe deeply and slowly, allowing your breath to massage your internal organs and release any stagnant energy.

As you complete this practice, take a moment to notice how your body feels. You may feel lighter, more spacious, or more grounded. The anger may not be completely gone, but it may feel less overwhelming. Remember that this is a process, and it may take time to fully release the anger held in your body. Be gentle with yourself, and trust in your body’s innate wisdom and capacity to heal.

## FAQ: Navigating Anger When You Miss a Funeral

**Q: Is it normal to feel so angry about missing a funeral?**
**A:** Yes, absolutely. Funerals are essential rituals that help us process grief and transition. When you are denied the opportunity to participate in this ritual, it is natural for your body to respond with anger. This anger is a protective mechanism, a way for your body to assert its need for acknowledgment and closure. It is not a sign of weakness or pathology; it is a biological response to an interrupted process.

**Q: How can I express this anger without hurting others?**
**A:** The key is to find healthy, embodied ways to express your anger. This might involve physical movement, such as running, dancing, or practicing yoga. It might involve creative expression, such as writing, painting, or playing music. It might also involve somatic practices, such as the one described in the previous section, which help you access and release the tension held in your muscles and fascia. The goal is not to suppress the anger, but to channel it in a way that honors your experience without causing harm.

**Q: What if the anger doesn't go away?**
**A:** Anger is a complex emotion, and it may take time to fully process and release it. If you find that your anger is persistent or overwhelming, it may be helpful to seek support from a therapist or counselor who specializes in somatic psychology or trauma. They can help you explore the underlying causes of your anger and develop strategies for managing it. Remember that healing is a journey, and it is okay to ask for help along the way.

**Q: Can herbs really help with this kind of anger?**
**A:** Yes, herbs can be a powerful ally in supporting the nervous system and easing muscular tension. Herbs like skullcap, chamomile, and lemon balm are known for their calming properties and can help soothe the physical symptoms of anger. However, it is important to remember that herbs are not a substitute for processing the underlying emotions. They are a supportive tool that can help create the conditions for healing, but the real work must be done through embodied practice and mindful awareness.

**Q: How can I create my own ritual to honor the person who died?**
**A:** Creating your own ritual can be a powerful way to find closure and honor the person you lost. This ritual can be as simple or as elaborate as you like. It might involve lighting a candle, saying a prayer, or writing a letter to the person who died. It might involve visiting a place that was special to them, or creating a small altar in your home. The most important thing is that the ritual feels meaningful and authentic to you. By creating your own ritual, you are reclaiming your right to grieve and honoring the connection you shared with the person who died.

## The Weight in the Psoas

The psoas muscle, often referred to as the "muscle of the soul," is a deep-seated core muscle that connects the lumbar spine to the pelvis and legs. It is intimately involved in our fight-or-flight response, and it is one of the first muscles to contract when we experience trauma or stress. When you miss a funeral, the grief and anger that you are unable to express publicly can become trapped in the psoas, leading to chronic tension and pain. This tension is not just physical; it is a somatic manifestation of the unresolved emotions surrounding the loss.

The diaphragm, which sits just above the psoas, is also deeply affected by grief and anger. When we are stressed or traumatized, our breathing often becomes shallow and rapid, which can cause the diaphragm to become tight and restricted. This restriction can further exacerbate the tension in the psoas, creating a vicious cycle of physical and emotional distress. The connection between the psoas and the diaphragm highlights the profound interplay between our physical bodies and our emotional experiences.

To release the tension in the psoas and diaphragm, it is essential to engage in somatic practices that promote relaxation and deep breathing. Gentle stretching, yoga, and mindful breathing exercises can help to lengthen and soften these muscles, allowing the trapped emotions to surface and be processed. It is also important to create a safe and supportive environment where you can explore and express your feelings without judgment. This might involve working with a somatic therapist, joining a support group, or simply finding a quiet space to reflect and journal.

As you begin to release the tension in your psoas and diaphragm, you may notice a shift in your emotional state. The anger and grief that have been held in your body may begin to dissipate, making way for a sense of peace and acceptance. This process is not always easy, and it may require time and patience. However, by honoring the wisdom of your body and engaging in somatic practices, you can begin to heal the deep-seated wounds of the missed funeral and find a path forward in your grief journey.

The journey of healing is not linear, and there will be times when the anger and grief resurface. When this happens, it is important to remember that these emotions are a natural part of the grieving process. Rather than trying to suppress or ignore them, try to approach them with curiosity and compassion. Listen to what your body is telling you, and allow yourself to feel whatever arises. By doing so, you are honoring the memory of the person you lost and taking an important step towards your own healing and wholeness.
